planting.note: Plant scarified seeds or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky, sandy or gravelly, lean soil in full sun; blue palo verde is extremely drought tolerant once established (essentially zero supplemental water in Sonoran Desert gardens after 2–3 years). Frost-sensitive in youth but tolerates brief temperatures to 15°F when mature.

directions: Plant scarified seeds or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky, sandy or gravelly, lean soil in full sun; blue palo verde is the state tree of Arizona and the signature large tree of the Sonoran Desert — a spectacularly beautiful, low-branched, naturally multi-stemmed small tree whose entire trunk, branches, and even branchlets are photosynthetically active and bright blue-green ('palo verde' = green stick in Spanish). The trunk synthesizes chlorophyll, allowing the tree to photosynthesize even when it drops its tiny leaves during drought. In spring, the tree produces a breathtaking, completely enveloping display of brilliant yellow flowers that cover every branch tip, creating one of the most spectacular natural flowering events in North American desert landscapes. The edible seeds (large, protein-rich) were a staple food for the Tohono O'odham and other desert peoples. Extremely drought tolerant and fast-growing for the desert. The flat seed pods are also edible when young and green. An essential native for Sonoran Desert gardens, xeriscape, and low-water landscape plantings in zones 8–11.

planting.note: Plant scarified seeds or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky, sandy or gravelly, lean soil in full sun; foothill palo verde is the smaller, more drought-resistant palo verde of rocky Sonoran Desert slopes and bajadas. More cold-hardy than blue palo verde, surviving to about 10°F when established. Extremely slow-growing on the rocky, dry sites it prefers.

directions: Plant scarified seeds or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky, sandy or gravelly, lean soil in full sun; foothill palo verde (yellow palo verde) is the smaller, stouter, more spiny, and more drought-resistant companion of blue palo verde in the Sonoran Desert — where both species often grow together on rocky desert slopes, bajadas, and wash margins. Distinguished from blue palo verde by its shorter stature, more yellow-green (rather than blue-green) trunk and branches, more prominent spines, and pale yellow flowers (slightly paler than blue palo verde's bright yellow). Like blue palo verde, the green trunk is photosynthetically active, allowing drought survival after leaf drop. Slower growing than blue palo verde and eventually smaller, making it appropriate for smaller garden spaces. The seeds are also edible. Produces the iconic Sonoran Desert 'nurse plant' relationship with saguaro cactus — more saguaro seedlings establish and survive under the nurse canopy of palo verde and other desert shrubs than in open desert. An essential Sonoran Desert native for xeriscape plantings in zone 8–11.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; blue dawn flower is frost-sensitive (killed to the ground by frost but often returning from roots in zone 8-9) and grows as a vigorous perennial in frost-free zone 9-11. Can become invasive in warm coastal climates. Listed as invasive in Hawaii and Florida.

directions: Plant cuttings or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; blue dawn flower (blue morning glory) is one of the most spectacularly beautiful ornamental vines for warm climates — producing abundant 3–4 inch diameter, vivid violet-blue to deep blue-purple morning glory flowers (changing color slightly through the day from bright blue in morning to blue-purple by afternoon) from spring through fall on a vigorous, twining vine that can grow 10–15 feet or more in a season. Unlike many morning glories, this is a perennial vine (in zones 9–11) that builds a woody base and becomes more vigorous each year. Spreads by self-seeding abundantly and can be invasive in warm coastal and subtropical climates — listed as invasive in Hawaii and Florida. A magnificent flowering vine for trellises, fences, and arbors in zones 8–11 where its aggressive nature can be managed. The vivid blue is particularly rare and striking among flowering vines. Hummingbirds visit the flowers.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average, loamy or clay-loam soil in full sun to partial shade; burning bush is adaptable to a wide range of soil conditions. NOTE: Burning bush is listed as invasive in many eastern and midwestern US states and should NOT be planted where native forest borders are present — it self-sows abundantly and displaces native vegetation.

directions: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average, loamy or clay-loam soil in full sun to partial shade; burning bush is one of the most widely planted ornamental shrubs in North America — famous for its extraordinary, blazing, neon-scarlet to crimson fall color, one of the most brilliant autumn displays of any deciduous shrub. The distinctive corky, wing-like ridges on the stems ('winged' euonymus) are immediately distinctive and attractive year-round. Extremely adaptable — tolerating a wide range of soils, light conditions, and drought. However, burning bush has escaped cultivation and naturalized in forests throughout the eastern and midwestern US, where it displaces native understory vegetation. Listed as invasive in Connecticut, Massachusetts, New Hampshire, and other states — banned from sale in some states. Native plant alternatives with comparable fall color include: native highbush blueberry (Vaccinium corymbosum) and black gum (Nyssa sylvatica). If planting, choose a dwarf, compact cultivar ('Compactus') away from natural areas and remove the small orange-red berries before birds can disperse the seeds.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy soil in full sun to full shade; Japanese euonymus is one of the most shade- and salt-tolerant evergreen shrubs available — tolerating deep shade, coastal salt spray, and air pollution. Highly susceptible to euonymus scale insect — monitor regularly and treat with horticultural oil as needed.

directions: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy soil in full sun to full shade; Japanese euonymus is one of the most widely used evergreen hedge and foundation shrubs in the southeastern US and mild-climate gardens worldwide — valued for its exceptional tolerance of deep shade, salt spray, air pollution, and a wide range of soil conditions. The thick, glossy, dark green leaves with finely toothed margins are attractive year-round, and the many cultivars offer gold-edged ('Aureomarginatus'), silver-edged ('Argenteomarginatus'), and yellow-centered ('Aureus') variegated leaf forms. A key advantage over many evergreen alternatives is its tolerance of deep shade — growing and maintaining dense, attractive foliage even in the shade of buildings or under tree canopies where most evergreen shrubs become thin and leggy. The main pest is euonymus scale (Unaspis euonymi) — small white and gray scale insects that can defoliate and kill plants if left untreated. An essential versatile evergreen for shaded foundation plantings, formal hedging, and coastal gardens in zones 6–9.
